author: @evolv_85

author: @andycarter4888
text:
I absolutely agree with your point, spot on, but for me, there's extra dimensions here.  In the course of building something, I come across problems that require that I create tools or jigs to get around and they become projects, or sub-projects in in them selves.  This fosters an interest in tools and as I often can't afford or justify high end kit, I usually wind up finishing a project by starting and finishing one or two others which were for the tools to finish the first project!  Now if I'd thrown a jig together for a single use and it came out well, then fine, but if it could be used in the future, then I suffer from the Diderot effect - the urge to update everything else to be as good as that one.  It never ends...
For what it's worth, my favorite tool is a €11 pocket lock knife with a standard blade and a Stanley holder.  It's so useful, I'd be lost without it!

author: @keXen
text:
A good and large toolset is very good to have, because it saves you a bunch of time. If you spend the extra time watching TV, then it's no good. If you spend your extra time being creative and taking your project two steps futer it's excellent.

You have freed up a lot of time to be creative and productive because of your 3D printer. Tools are great if they enhances your product, not if they makes you lazy.

author: @ogi22
text:
Your example with Tinkercad is very profound. This tool helps people to learn how to make compicated objects from very simple basic shapes. Something that every good machinist must learn. If you then start using proffesional CAD programs, you will have a foundation layer which will help to be much more efficient in your work.
This reminded me one aeroplane accident. The pilot were able to glide a big, heavy Boeing very far and land safely just because he was also a good glider pilot. He understood basics of flying. He also said later that in his oppinion, every pilot should also learn how to fly a glider.

author: @PatrickGunderson
text: For me, Tinkercad is missing 4 main functions that make it non-viable for a CAD workflow: extrude, revolve, loft and sweep. Mr Murray-Smith, I started with tinkercad as well and I bet I could get you to the same capability you have using tinkercad, using fusion360 in about 30 minutes. Would you be willing to give me a half-hour to try?

author: @stewiex
text:
I'm not your target demo with this video as I'm a computer  nerd who gets enjoyment out of learning computer programs just as much as using them. I like to give options to people asking for program advice though when it comes to CAD. 

Tinkercad is on the list of recommendations, along with Fusion 360, OnShape and even Microsoft Paint 3D which is installed by default on all new Windows PCs. 

I agree Tinkercad is great though and designed in a way that tries to make it simple for anyone to pick up. For anyone who may find some of the other free options intimidating, it is a great choice and just as capable.

Really the one tool a person chooses just depends on their needs. You can accomplish the same thing with any of them, some just need more tinkering than others depending on what they are trying to make.

P.S. Despite being a computer nerd, I still make a lot out of wood, metal, pla, etc and would make even more if I didn't work a busy job and have 2 small kids. 😅

replies.author: @stewiex
replies.text: Also, I went many years with just a circular saw and made about every possible cut with it.... But you couldn't pay me enough money to sell my Mitre saw. Maybe my Table saw and Reciprocating saw... But sometimes the convenience of better tools wins out when you have limited time to fiddle with just one. 😂

author: @mcmillanfarms
text: I use Fusion 360 at home for design work for CNC based machinery. At work I have to get into Autocad more but to your point, Tinkercad is GREAT. I was playing around with it with my kids and was amazed at how simple it was to create complex shapes and models. I made one of those ball in a box nick-nacks in less than 3 minutes on Tinkercad and decided to see how long it took to do the same in Fusion. It took me almost 10 minutes to create the same thing in Fusion. Yes, it had more "defined" parameters in Fusion but who cares. The Fusion version looked exactly like the Tinkercad version when I printed it.

author: @programmingfortheweb
text:
Erm, hornet's nest. As a hobby, I hand make small items (wallets, bags, pouches, belts etc.) out of vegetable tanned leather. I started with very cheap Chinese (yes they were Chinese) tools. Now some of these tools are very usable and I still use them today. But some, nah, they were terrible. Those that were terrible I replaced with mid range tools and the difference is chalk and cheese.

I do agree that you only need a relatively small set of tools, For example I mainly use a knife, pricking irons, a maul, needles, a right angle ruler, some emery board, some hole punches and tools for setting snaps/rivets. Yes, I have others but only use them occasionally.

author: @garyhardman8369
text:
Very true Rob.
I'm 63 now and have always loved tinkering and building 'stuff'.
As a child of about eight years old, I loved making simple go carts, amongst other things.
We had no internet, all my solutions had to come from my own imagination.
I would go to the local tip and salvage pram wheels and axles. To fasten the wooden pieces together, I would salvage nails from random lumps of timber and straighten them with a hammer.
Fast forward to today, and I own a 3D printer, a small metalworking lathe and many corded and cordless power tools.
I am grateful that I grew up in an era that made me use my own initiative!

author: @jasonburguess
text: I live off grid in rural Alaska, and I use two tools to build almost everything: a chainsaw and a hammer, I've build whole buildings using literally nothing else, and not just when working with logs, but standard dimensional lumber too, the chainsaw takes the place of : circular saw,  jigsaw, reciprocating saw, table saw, router, etc. And never needs to be recharged or plugged in, and the hammer replaces: impact driver, screw gun, nail gun, etc. Bucket of nails, and some wood, and I can have a building up with a roof by myself in one day.

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: I agree and would add use what you are familiar with.  I have done programming so OpenSCAD is a lot like programming.  you put in a box, give it dimensions to make it a flat rectangle, put in a cylinder, give it dimensions, have it at 4 places and subtracted from the box and you have a name plate with holes for mounting.  Most people are afraid as it is ' new to them ' and not look at it as what are the simple parts ( or tools ) to make the widget they are after.

author: @jb-dn3ct
text:
I'll admit I'm guilty of not getting a round tuit, I get the needs must things sorted with urgency then I get some play time continuing the garage workshop build, then something happens like the awd system on the car stoping working the other week so off the ground stip things down, found splines worn on a coupler, got the new one did some grinding then some drilling then some welding then some re building to finish with a much stronger setup.

As much as I get done though I know I can always make time for more things, I could be doing something now instead of writing this for a start but I do need some down time.

Going on about tools though I have my tools I need, I have my tools I want to get but more importantly I have a huge old bag in the garage with all of my broken and surplus bits and bobs, some are probably older than me, I keep these bits because I can and do make use of them, for instance I had and old adjustable flexy pipe wrench, the adjustable metal section was all deformed through hard use, I cut the flex head off and welded it to some square bar and welded a half inch socket on the other end and it's saved me from needing to buy a crows foot wrench, i have old broken breaker bars that are ground down to use as pry bars and one just cut to use as a huge punch, that bag of old bits has been a life saver over the years, yes I do add tools but any tool even broken ones can be made useful

author: @jonbutcher9805
text: People buy tools on the odd chance they want to do or build stuff. Frustrating to want to do something and not having the tool to do the job. Having to buy a this or that or worse to borrow something is just another chore in an already full day. Most people I know love getting stuff whether they need it or not the having is the thing for the just in case. That's why garages and workshops are filled with all kinds of shiny unused but always ready to be used things and I'm sure Robert and Luke are no different. Price is the only limiting factor. That's when birthday, father's day and Christmas day wishlists are looked forward too. Not because they're needed but the just maybe someday usage. All men are the same in this regard... Hmm. Yes!!  Limited means, sure. That's why Boot sales or Yard sales are eagerly ravaged over by the weekend tinkerer's. The last 30 odd years have seen a gaggle of big box hardware stores and online retail sites explode for those exact reasons and monthly payments lesser the guilty splurges. Yes it's nice if you can do stuff with a handful of tools, but honestly we're not taught or shown how to do or build and even repair that way. It's always hammered( pun ) home that you need the right tool for the right job. If you can do stuff with limited items that's awesome. But most of us live in the have as much as you can world whether needed or not. Mostly not. LOL

author: @newmonengineering
text: I do a ton of tinkering as well. I have acquired a ton of tools but mostly because they make a particular job possible. That said I enjoy doing things, I feel like I accomplished something in the end. Sometimes I enjoy trying a few different tools to find the one I prefer the most, I think about other ways to use the same tool and that makes a big difference as well. You could probably spin circles around me on tinker cad, but I could probably do the same to you on Fusion 360. And the reason is we each learned our tool well. The more time you spend on using the tool the better you can make it do what you want!!

author: @jizburg
text:
As a car mecanic i know there are a few tools that a MUST have, then there are THOUSANDS! of things that is nice to have but you dont need.

Example: a hydraulic press to press bearings with. Its a good tool. But the job can be done with a hammer, and an iron rod to get the old bearing out of there. Then put the new bearing in the freezer and heat upp the hole the bearing goes into with a blowtorch and just drop it in and tap it a few times with the iron rod to get it seated correctly.

author: @cliffmullen6751
text: A Round Tuit... LOL I haven't heard that one in a very long time

author: @Jams848484
text: I'm an accomplished computer programmer with ~20 years of experience. I'm also a hobbyist wood worker with about a years worth of weekends under my belt. My workshop is immaculate and full of the best tools I can afford (some unused), but my computer is a neglected and abused wreck. Most of the letters have worn away on my keyboard and I have to touch-type. My fancy Japanese ryoba saw has never been used (I'm scared to get a scratch on it).

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, you need more tools than 3 to fix a car. Sockets, torque wrenches, and other specialized tools may be required to safely repair the automobile. Now computerized readers, and diagnostic tools may be in order.

author: @industrialmonk
text: I was told when I was an apprentice what would you do if you were in a desert (my answer but I'm in Bolton Lancashire got a slap) & then had to repair the machine with just what I had. required a large amount of ingenuity but I great lesson.

author: @evanburroughs9329
text: Spot on!

author: @grendel1960a
text:
totally agree, its very rare I buy a brand new tool, the majority have been picked up at car boot fairs (some of the older tools are better quality anyway) for me part of the fun of a new (to me) tool is actually stripping it down and getting it to function properly. i do occasionally procrastinate, bt i try and get something done each and every day. I do like a better CAD package though, its what I have trained with, something I have been professionally building my skills with for over 4 decades, so I use what I know best in that instance. yes I do have a lot off tools, thats because in reality I turn my hands to a lot of different things. I do follow adam savages maxim, when you first buy a tool, get the cheapest you can find, and if you use it a lot, when you replace it do so with the best you can afford. because you use it a lot. for me one day I can decide to hand knot a rug, i might even spin the wool to do so, then another day its tinkering some part, recently I 3d printed a replacement bracket for my tablet case, now the 3d part has broken (again) its time to look at producing a more durable version, i am considering making it out of aluminium, on the mill, for a part that is not going to break as quick.
as such I am a bit of a Jack of all Trades, once a 3d printed prototype works, I can then proceed to produce a more durable replica. my problem is too many tinkering ideas, too little time, fitting it all around 8 hours  day working. but a little bit every day.

I simplified what I have and now enjoy building  even more. You're definately on to something here.

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
I largely agree with exception only where the tool IS the job AND you've mastered the quality-but-mid-range one first and this "premium example" is to allow you to do the work faster OR more comfortably.
For the one I'm not using all day, every day, the $25 hammer will probably serve me for the rest of my life.

author: @flemmingjacobsen2763
text: Reminds me of "the law of large hammers".... given a sufficiently large hammer, any two objects can be made to fit....😅

replies.author: @timothyneiswander3151
replies.text:
The same law could be applied to seperating one object from another.

This law must not be well known. Everytime I see someone having difficulty working on something I ask them if they need a bigger hammer and I always get a blank stare.

replies.author: @dr.froghopper6711
replies.text: I knew a guy in the Navy named Vargo. He developed what he called The Vargo Technique for repairing faulty equipment. It went like this: 1) doesn’t work? 2) give it 10-20 licks with your biggest hammer.3) still doesn’t work? 4) it’s broken for sure! Toss it!

author: @FormerlyKnownAsAndrew
text:
I do atleast one thing on my projects each day. I dont care what it is....a sketch, layout, cutlist, actual cuts, a glue up, part of an assembly... whatever it is... ATLEAST one thing per day.

If all I have time for is a single cut ... I'll do it any be happy because I moved my vision forward.

This video hits on a different level. Im currently 3d printing a paint rack for my supplies. 

I'm painting a wood rack jiggle maker.

author: @christianriera2489
text: There was an informative, ongoing documentary a few decades back about using what tools you've got: MacGyver. ;p

replies.author: @matthewellisor5835
replies.text:
When all you have it's a hammer, not everything need look like a nail.
I've seen one of the best carpenters that I know, use his framing hammer's claw to perfectly notch a 2"×4" (we primarily build with wood around here) while framing up a very large and expensive house. As if that wasn't enough to impress, he did it with only one foothold and at full extension to reach.
A few chop, chops and a wack with the head, drops the hammer into it's place on the tool belt and smoothly slides the next piece of lumber into it's new home.
I find it inspiring to see masters at work.
